summary.objective: The paper investigates how geometric disorder from randomly distributed static obstacles on a two-dimensional square lattice influences collective chase-and-escape dynamics.
summary.methods: The authors employed agent-based simulations with short-range sensing rules, Breadth-First Search for connectivity analysis, and measured trapping time, capture cost, survival statistics via Weibull distribution, and mean-squared displacement exponent.
summary.results: Results show non-monotonic dependencies of trapping time and capture cost on obstacle density with a maximum near the percolation threshold; survival decay follows a Weibull distribution exhibiting crossovers from compressed to stretched exponential; and transport displays sub-diffusive behavior converging to a geometry-controlled regime near percolation.
summary.conclusion: The study concludes that chase-escape dynamics in disordered environments are governed by a geometry-driven crossover, where percolation and lattice connectivity act as unifying control parameters for spatial, temporal, and collective behavior.
